name: Esther
description: Esther, also known as Hadassah, is the central figure in the Book of Esther. A Jewish woman living in Persia during the reign of King Ahasuerus (believed to be Xerxes I), she was chosen to be queen after Ahasuerus deposed Queen Vashti. Esther concealed her Jewish identity on the advice of her cousin and guardian, Mordecai. When Haman, the king's advisor, plots to annihilate all the Jews in the Persian Empire, Mordecai persuades Esther to reveal her true identity to the king and plead for her people. Esther bravely risks her life by approaching the king uninvited, a crime punishable by death. She cleverly exposes Haman's plot during a banquet, leading to Haman's execution and the salvation of the Jewish people. Her courage, quick thinking, and unwavering loyalty to her people have made her a symbol of hope, resilience, and female leadership within Jewish tradition. The holiday of Purim commemorates Esther's bravery and the Jewish people's deliverance. Esther's story is a testament to the power of faith, courage, and the importance of standing up for what is right, even in the face of overwhelming adversity. Her legacy continues to inspire generations to fight for justice and equality.
subtitle: Saved her people from annihilation.
achievements: Saved the Jewish people from genocide

keyMyths.title: Esther Becomes Queen
keyMyths.description: After Queen Vashti is deposed, Esther is chosen for her beauty and becomes the new Queen of Persia. She keeps her Jewish identity a secret as instructed by Mordecai.

keyMyths.title: Haman's Plot
keyMyths.description: Haman, angered by Mordecai's refusal to bow, plots to kill all the Jews in the Persian Empire. He convinces King Ahasuerus to issue a decree for their annihilation.

keyMyths.title: Esther Saves Her People
keyMyths.description: Esther bravely reveals her Jewish identity to the king and exposes Haman's plot during a banquet. Haman is executed, and the Jews are saved from destruction.

sources.author: Anonymous
sources.description: The primary source for the story of Esther.
sources.text: The primary source for the story of Esther.

sources.author: Talmudic Scholars
sources.description: Contains rabbinic interpretations and commentary on the Book of Esther.
sources.text: Contains rabbinic interpretations and commentary on the Book of Esther.

associations.name: Myrtle (Hadassah)
associations.significance: Esther's Hebrew name, Hadassah, means myrtle, symbolizing beauty, peace, and immortality.

associations.name: Hidden Identity
associations.significance: Represents the concealed Jewish identity of Esther and the hidden hand of God working through her.

associations.name: Megillat Esther
associations.significance: The Scroll of Esther, read annually on Purim, recounts her story and commemorates the Jewish people's salvation.

associations.name: Queenship
associations.significance: Symbolizes Esther's position of power and influence within the Persian court, which she uses to protect her people.

associations.name: Fasting and Prayer
associations.significance: Represents the Jewish people's reliance on divine intervention and their collective plea for deliverance.

associations.name: Banquet of Esther
associations.significance: Symbolizes Esther's strategic use of diplomacy and hospitality to expose Haman's plot and sway the king.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both Persephone and Esther are figures who navigate powerful, potentially dangerous courts and influence the fate of many.
Like Moses, Esther acts as an intercessor for her people, risking her own life to plead for their salvation from annihilation.
Both Esther and Cloelia demonstrate courage and agency in the face of powerful political forces, acting to save their people.

worshipPractices.name: Reading of the Megillah
worshipPractices.description: The public reading of the Scroll of Esther (Megillat Esther) is the central ritual of Purim. It is read aloud in synagogues, and listeners are encouraged to make noise whenever Haman's name is mentioned.

worshipPractices.name: Purim Feast
worshipPractices.description: A festive meal (Seudat Purim) is held on Purim, often including traditional foods like hamantaschen (filled triangular pastries). The feast celebrates the Jews' deliverance from Haman's plot.

worshipPractices.name: Mishloach Manot
worshipPractices.description: The sending of gifts of food (Mishloach Manot) to friends and neighbors is a custom on Purim, promoting goodwill and community spirit.

worshipPractices.name: Matanot La'evyonim
worshipPractices.description: Giving charity to the poor (Matanot La'evyonim) is a mitzvah (commandment) on Purim, ensuring that everyone can participate in the celebration.

cultural.modernLegacy: Esther's story continues to inspire as a symbol of courage, resilience, and the power of individuals to effect change. Her narrative is often invoked in discussions of Jewish identity, persecution, and the importance of standing up for one's community. The holiday of Purim, celebrating Esther's triumph, remains a vibrant and joyous occasion for Jewish people worldwide.

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Ascent to Queenship
extendedContent.content: Esther, originally named Hadassah, was a young Jewish woman living in exile in Persia. Orphaned at a young age, she was raised by her cousin Mordecai, who instilled in her a strong sense of Jewish identity and values. When King Ahasuerus sought a new queen after Queen Vashti's defiance, Esther was among the women brought before him. Guided by Mordecai, she concealed her Jewish heritage. Esther's beauty and grace captivated the king, and she was chosen as his queen. Her position in the royal court placed her in a unique position to influence events, though she remained loyal to her upbringing. The narrative emphasizes Esther's obedience to Mordecai's instructions, highlighting the importance of mentorship and guidance in her life. This period of Esther's life lays the foundation for her later acts of courage and self-sacrifice.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests: Foiling Haman's Plot
extendedContent.content: Esther's most significant act was her intervention to save the Jewish people from annihilation. Haman, the king's advisor, harbored a deep hatred for the Jews and manipulated Ahasuerus into issuing a decree ordering their extermination. Mordecai learned of the plot and implored Esther to use her position to intercede with the king. Initially hesitant due to the danger of approaching the king uninvited, Esther ultimately resolved to act. She fasted and prayed, and then bravely approached Ahasuerus. Esther skillfully revealed Haman's treachery during a series of banquets, exposing his plan to destroy the Jews. Her eloquence and courage swayed the king, who then ordered Haman's execution and allowed the Jews to defend themselves. This sequence of events demonstrates Esther's strategic thinking, her ability to navigate the complexities of the Persian court, and her unwavering commitment to her people.

extendedContent.title: Legacy and Significance
extendedContent.content: Esther's legacy is celebrated annually through the festival of Purim, a joyous occasion marked by the reading of the Scroll of Esther, feasting, and the exchange of gifts. Her story serves as a powerful reminder of the importance of courage, resilience, and faith in the face of adversity. Esther is revered as a symbol of Jewish survival and a testament to the power of individuals to make a difference. Her narrative has been interpreted in various ways throughout history, with some focusing on her role as a model of female leadership and others emphasizing her piety and obedience. Regardless of interpretation, Esther remains a significant figure in Jewish history and culture, inspiring generations with her unwavering commitment to her people. Her tale also highlights themes of hidden identity, divine providence, and the triumph of good over evil.

shortDescription: Esther, a Jewish queen of Persia, bravely risked her life to save her people from annihilation, an event commemorated by the festival of Purim.
